message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Natively-recorded fast speech is LESS intelligible than a normal-rate voice that is linearly time-compressed to the same rate, suggesting post-hoc time-scale modification of a normal voice can beat naturally-produced fast speech for intelligibility — relevant to the native-fast-generation vs. time-compression trade-off (angle 3)."

**Source:** https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Let me fetch the primary source to verify the supporting quote and check the claim.

input.prompt: What is this paper about? Does it state that unprocessed/naturally-produced fast speech is less intelligible than linearly time-compressed normal speech? Quote any relevant findings about fast speech intelligibility, time compression, and comparisons between natural fast speech and time-compressed normal speech. What are the exact conditions and rates tested? Is this about TTS/synthetic speech or natural human speech?

input.query: natural fast speech less intelligible than time-compressed normal speech

content.content:
# Summary of Paper arXiv:1901.07239

## Topic
This paper investigates non-linear time compression methods applied to different speech styles, comparing intelligibility outcomes across natural and synthetically modified speech.

## Key Finding on Fast Speech
Yes, the paper confirms that naturally-produced fast speech showed reduced intelligibility. According to the abstract:

> "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech"

Additionally:

> "Fast speech was also less intelligible than compressed clear speech but at the highest rate (three times faster than normal) the advantage of clear over fast speech was lost"

## Speech Type
This research concerns **natural human speech**, not synthetic/TTS speech. The study examines "clear and normal speech" styles that were either naturally fast or artificially time-compressed.

## Compression Rates and Methods
The experiments tested:
- Linear (uniform) compression methods
- Non-linear methods compressing speech and silence at different rates
- A highest rate condition of **three times faster than normal** speech
- A method compressing silence twice as much as speech

The research demonstrates that compressed normal speech remained more intelligible than compressed clear speech, even when controlling for overall duration.

Web search results for query: "natural fast speech less intelligible than time-compressed normal speech"

Links: [{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222400427_Word_perception_in_fast_speech_Artificially_time-compressed_vs_naturally_produced_fast_speech"},{"title":"Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/293804518_Natural_fast_speech_is_perceived_as_faster_than_linearly_time-compressed_speech"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Max Planck Institute","url":"https://www.mpi.nl/publications/item66910/word-perception-fast-speech-artificially-time-compressed-vs-naturally"},{"title":"Does Time Compression Decrease Intelligibility for Female Talkers More Than for Male Talkers? - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7242980/"},{"title":"Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech | Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092"},{"title":"Adaptation to natural fast speech and time-compressed speech in children","url":"https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2013/guiraud13_interspeech.pdf"},{"title":"(PDF) Review of research on the intelligibility and comprehension of accelerated speech","url":"https://www.academia.edu/115224083/Review_of_research_on_the_intelligibility_and_comprehension_of_accelerated_speech"},{"title":"Too fast to be true? Exploring time compression in simultaneous interpreting - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315001041"}]

Based on the search results, I found substantial research on this topic. Here's what the evidence shows:

Unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech. This finding has been consistently documented across multiple studies.

The key reason for this difference relates to articulation quality. Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech. More specifically, natural fast speech is even less intelligible than TC speech, partly because natural fast speech results in reduced vowel space.

Additionally, in natural fast speech, not all segments are compressed equally—vowels tend to shorten relatively more than consonants, and unstressed syllables get shortened to a greater extent or are more likely to be deleted than stressed syllables. Despite this, linearly compressed fast speech appeared to be most intelligible, and although listeners were able to adapt to and improve perception of both types of speech, transfer of improved understanding occurred only from linearly compressed speech to natural fast speech but not the other way around.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

The claim says it's "relevant to the native-fast-generation vs. time-compression trade-off (angle 3)" — it does not over-assert that this is direct TTS evidence; it frames it as relevant/suggestive. Let me confirm there's no major contradiction and assess the strength.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "time-compressed synthetic speech intelligibility compression method speaking style fast vs normal"

Links: [{"title":"The effects of time compression on the comprehension of natural and synthetic speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228339419_The_effects_of_time_compression_on_the_comprehension_of_natural_and_synthetic_speech"},{"title":"(PDF) Intelligibility analysis of fast synthesized speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/263487249_Intelligibility_analysis_of_fast_synthesized_speech"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282424493_Intelligibility_of_time-compressed_synthetic_speech_Compression_method_and_speaking_style"},{"title":"On the comprehension of extremely fast synthetic speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/277991598_On_the_comprehension_of_extremely_fast_synthetic_speech"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222400427_Word_perception_in_fast_speech_Artificially_time-compressed_vs_naturally_produced_fast_speech"},{"title":"Speech Intelligibility Enhancement Using Non-Parallel Speaking Style Conversion With Stargan And Dynamic Range Compression | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/342604472_Speech_Intelligibility_Enhancement_Using_Non-Parallel_Speaking_Style_Conversion_With_Stargan_And_Dynamic_Range_Compression"},{"title":"Techniques, Perception, and Applications of Time-Compressed Speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/2885840_Techniques_Perception_and_Applications_of_Time-Compressed_Speech"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found on time-compressed synthetic speech intelligibility:

## Research Overview

Research has examined intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech with experiments performed on natural and synthetic speech time-compressed at various rates, analyzing the effect of speech corpus and compression method on intelligibility scores.

## Compression Methods

Three compression methods were compared: scaling the variance of the state duration model, interpolating the duration models of the fast and normal voices, and applying a linear compression method to the generated speech waveform.

## Speaking Style Impact

The research directly compared how normal versus fast speaking styles affect intelligibility:

Voice talents recorded prompts at normal and fast rates, and statistical parametric speech synthesis systems were trained using the normal and fast data of each speaker.

## Key Findings

For English voices, generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates. For German voices, interpolation was found to be better at moderate speaking rates but the linear method was again more successful at very high rates, particularly when applied to the fast data.

The German speaker was able to reproduce speech at a fast rate with fewer deletion and substitution errors compared to the English speaker, supporting the intelligibility benefits observed when compressing his fast speech.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
This is decisive. The synthetic-speech finding (ScienceDirect S0167639315000977) directly supports the claim even in the TTS domain: "For English voices, generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates."

Let me assess the full picture:

**Claim assessment:**
1. **Supported by quote?** Yes, exactly. The arXiv abstract states verbatim "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech."
2. **Contradicting evidence?** None found. Instead, multiple independent studies corroborate (Janse et al. 2003, the 2016 Springer perception study, and critically the synthetic-speech study which found compressing a normal-rate voice beats fast-recorded data for English voices at all rates).
3. **Source quality?** Strong. Primary peer-reviewed work; the arXiv paper appears to be a preprint of the Speech Communication study. Convergent literature across 2003–2019.
4. **Outdated?** This is psychoacoustics of human speech perception — stable science, not a fast-moving benchmark. No staleness concern.
5. **Marketing/cherry-pick?** No, academic peer-reviewed sources.

**One important caveat (qualification, not refutation):** The arXiv finding is about *natural human* fast speech, not neural TTS native-fast generation. The claim correctly hedges by saying it is "relevant to" and "suggesting" — it does not over-assert it's direct TTS evidence. And the synthetic-speech study extends the conclusion to TTS for English voices (with a nuance: a skilled German speaker who articulated cleanly at speed did better, meaning the result is partly talker/articulation-dependent, not absolute). This nuance qualifies but does not refute the claim.

The claim survives all refutation attempts. It is well-supported, current, and backed by primary peer-reviewed sources, with the application-domain (TTS) version independently confirmed.

input.evidence: The claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source. arXiv:1901.07239 (a preprint of work in Speech Communication on time-compressed speech) states in its abstract: "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech." This is exactly the claim. The finding is corroborated by multiple independent peer-reviewed studies: (a) Janse et al., "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) found natural fast speech is less intelligible, partly due to reduced vowel space and uneven (non-linear) segment shortening; (b) the 2016 Springer perception study (10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x) found natural fast speech is perceived as faster and harder to process than linearly compressed speech; transfer of perceptual adaptation only went from compressed to natural, not vice versa. CRUCIALLY for the TTS/synthetic application domain, the study "Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639315000977) found that "generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates" for English voices — directly extending the claim to synthetic/TTS speech and supporting the time-compression-beats-native-fast conclusion. No contradicting credible source was found. QUALIFICATIONS (do not refute, but bound the claim): (1) The arXiv finding concerns NATURAL human speech, not neural-TTS native-fast generation; the claim correctly hedges ("relevant to," "suggesting") rather than over-asserting TTS evidence. (2) The synthetic study adds a talker-dependent nuance: a skilled German speaker who articulated cleanly at fast rate (few deletions/substitutions) yielded intelligible results when his fast data was compressed, meaning the advantage of "normal-then-compress" is not absolute but depends on how cleanly the fast version is articulated. Source quality (peer-reviewed Speech Communication / Springer), recency-stability (psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving benchmark), and convergent multi-study support all match the claim's strength. Sources: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x

content.text:
I've completed the verification. The claim survives all refutation attempts and is well-supported.

**Verdict: NOT refuted (refuted=false), high confidence.**

The claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source (arXiv:1901.07239), corroborated by three additional independent peer-reviewed studies, and — most importantly — independently confirmed in the actual TTS/synthetic-speech application domain by the Speech Communication study (S0167639315000977), which found that generating at normal rate then linearly compressing was the most intelligible approach at all tested rates for English voices.

The claim is appropriately hedged ("relevant to," "suggesting") so it does not over-extend the natural-speech finding to neural TTS. The only qualifications are bounding nuances (talker/articulation dependence), not refutations.
